Let me take you back…

Results Day. August 2022.

  • Celebrating the end of exams in Tuscany, with a school friend.
  • The night before — a video to our future selves.
  • That morning: a quiet room, and a little prayer.
Results Day · The moment

I needed A*AA for Oxford

Economics & Management — my dream course. I was quietly confident. Then I opened the email…

AMathematics
ABiology
BEconomics
Results Day · The decision

So I bet on myself

  • People told me not to — my mum included.
  • But I knew the grades I was capable of.
  • The plan: resit, and reapply to Oxford.
Christ's Hospital · Apply Day

Gap Years.

My results didn't go to plan — and that changed everything, for the better.

  • I'm God'sFavour — reading BSc Management at the LSE (third year).
  • Co-founder of The Steps Foundation, helping low-income and first-generation students.
  • Most recently, an investment banking summer analyst at Lazard ('25).
  • That gap year you just heard the start of? Let me walk you through it.

God'sFavour Oluwanusin

LSE · The Steps Foundation · IB Summer Analyst, Lazard '25

Hospitality work, A-level retakes, finance programmes and a fair bit of travel — all in one year out.

The basics

So, what is a gap year?

A year out between school and the next step — university, an apprenticeship, work, or whatever comes after.

  • A pause to work, travel, retake exams, reapply, or simply reset.
  • Some are carefully planned. Others arrive completely uninvited.
  • Either way — it is not wasted time, and it is not the end of the world.
The two kinds

There are really two types

Type 1 · Voluntary

You chose it

Whatever your grades, you'd already decided to take a year out — to explore a passion, volunteer abroad, work, or just take some time for yourself. Totally fine, and a real privilege.

Type 2 · Involuntary

It chose you

You didn't plan one. Then results day comes, and the options you wanted are suddenly limited by your grades. Not the plan — but far from a dead end.

An honest word

I get it — I was a sceptic too

  • I sat where you are, and almost scoffed at talks like this.
  • "Won't be me. Straight to uni."
  • Life comes quick — it's worth planning for the possibility.
How it actually works

The mechanics: two routes

Planned ahead

Deferred entry

You apply through UCAS in Year 13 as normal, but ask to start a year later. You hold a confirmed place and take your gap year with total certainty. The classic voluntary route.

After results

Reapplying in your gap year

You apply fresh in the next UCAS cycle — often with achieved (not predicted) grades behind you. The route many involuntary gappers take. It's exactly what I did.

And on results day itself — there's Clearing. If you don't hold an offer (or want to change), Clearing matches you to universities with spaces left: you find courses on UCAS, call the university directly, and if they say yes you add it as your choice. It runs from results day through the summer — a real, normal route in, not a last resort.

A key fork in the road

Retaking A-levels — or not?

With retakes

You re-sit exams to lift your predicted and final grades — to win back offers, open new doors, and strengthen your CV for years to come. It takes real discipline.

Without retakes

Your grades are settled, so the year is about experience, applications, work and growth rather than the classroom. A different kind of productive.

I retook three. I left school with a B and wanted better — better offers, and better grades on my CV going into university and beyond.

My year, briefly

July 2022 → September 2023

Earned & saved. Part-time agency hospitality work — ad hoc shifts for restaurants — to build up some money.

Reapplied & aimed higher. Re-won my Oxford offer (Economics & Management, Merton College) after missing it the first time.

Retook three A-levels. Better predictions, and better final grades (AAA) — my B became an A.

Built the CV. Morgan Stanley Step In, Step Up and Rothschild & Co Pioneer Programme — and met brilliant people.

Saw the world. Interrailed across Europe, plus family travel.

Travel, specifically

Where the year took me

Rome 🇮🇹
Petra, Jordan 🇯🇴
Venice 🇮🇹
Venice 🇮🇹 Zurich 🇨🇭 Munich 🇩🇪 Vienna 🇦🇹 Paris 🇫🇷 Budapest 🇭🇺 Jeddah 🇸🇦 Amman 🇯🇴 Wadi Rum 🇯🇴 Rome 🇮🇹
What a year can hold

The possibilities

💷

Earn money

Part-time or full-time work, and real independence that comes with it.

🎓

Reapply

Aim for offers and scholarships you couldn't the first time around.

📚

Retake exams

Lift your grades and reopen doors you thought had closed.

💼

Work experience

Internships and programmes that build your CV and network.

✈️

Travel

Interrailing, family trips — broaden your horizons.

🧭

Reset

Breathe, reflect, and start the next chapter on your own terms.

What I learned · 01

Independence & accountability

  • No schedule, no teachers checking in — all on me.
  • I rebuilt my own timetable in Google Calendar.
  • Turns out the school day exists for a reason.
What I learned · 02

Maturity & perspective

  • The less-trodden path broadens you.
  • More patience, more perspective.
  • A year older than your peers — and it shows.
What I learned · 03

Backing yourself pays off

  • I was told not to take the year — I did it anyway.
  • I came out with more clarity, better grades, a better offer.
  • Proof that betting on yourself works — what a lesson to learn early.
The hard parts · 01

The world feels like it's moving on

  • Friends in freshers', first lectures, new friends.
  • It's tough to watch — I won't pretend otherwise.
  • Your time will come.
The hard parts · 02

Everyone has their own timeline

  • The setback comes for almost everyone — sometime.
  • Internships, return offers, the job hunt, exams…
  • Yours just came earlier. It balances out.
The hard parts · 03

Loneliness & the friendship test

  • You'll be in the minority.
  • Proximity vs. who actually shows up.
  • Be intentional — keep good people close.
A hidden upside

Room to pivot

  • Space to ask: is this really the path I want?
  • People switch — PPE → History, medicine → geography. It happens.
  • You rarely need to study X to work in X — finance, law and software all take other degrees (conversion courses exist).
  • Above all: you have to enjoy your degree.
The takeaway

A gap year isn't falling behind. It's a different way forward.

Plan for the path you want — and don't fear the one you didn't.

Thank you · God'sFavour Oluwanusin · Questions welcome
